phase_detection.complete: (document.body && document.body.textContent && document.body.textContent.indexOf('Thank you for your participation') !== -1)

timing.trial_context_js: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if(!el) return ''; var name=(el.src||'').split('/').pop().replace('.png',''); var bi=(typeof block_ind!=='undefined')?block_ind:''; var ti=(typeof trial_ind!=='undefined')?trial_ind:''; var ssd=(typeof SSD!=='undefined')?SSD:''; return 'block='+bi+' trial='+ti+' stim='+name+' SSD='+ssd;})()

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, Brockett AT, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in unselected adult samples on STOP-IT-style two-choice tasks typically falls in the 480-540 ms range; ex-Gaussian decomposition places mu near 400-430 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Across choice-RT studies the Gaussian mode (mu) of the ex-Gaussian sits roughly 80-130 ms below the mean RT, with values typically 380-440 ms for two-choice visual discrimination.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in their validation sample of healthy young adults was approximately 500 ms with the standard 250 ms fixation / 1000-1250 ms MAXRT timing.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for choice-RT data cluster around 25-55 ms, reflecting Gaussian-component noise on the leading edge of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJ
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Across Stroop and choice-RT conditions, sigma typically falls between 25 and 50 ms in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau estimates for two-choice RT range from approximately 60 to 130 ms, with longer tau associated with attentional lapses and slow decision drift.

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJ
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau values in choice-RT typically lie in the 70-120 ms range and absorb the right-tail variability that the Gaussian alone cannot capture.

go.rationale: Young healthy adults on STOP-IT-style two-choice arrow discrimination produce mean correct go-RTs of ~480-520 ms. Ex-Gaussian decomposition typically partitions this into mu ~400-430 ms (Gaussian mode reflecting motor/sensory baseline), sigma ~30-50 ms (decisional noise on the Gaussian component), and tau ~80-110 ms (exponential right tail driven by occasional attentional lapses and decisional variability). Values selected mid-range reflect the consensus STOP-IT validation samples (Verbruggen et al., 2019) and ex-Gaussian fits in Matzke et al. for choice-RT under simple visual discrimination.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Under the independent horse-race model, signal-respond RTs are necessarily faster than the unconditional go-RT distribution because they correspond to trials on which the go process won the race.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Failed-stop (signal-respond) mean RT is consistently 30-100 ms shorter than correct-go RT in healthy samples.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The model assumption that signal-respond RT is faster than go-RT is a basic check on horse-race independence and is empirically supported across STOP-IT validation samples.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Truncating the go-RT distribution by selecting trials below the finishing time of the stop process yields a signal-respond distribution with similar local dispersion but reduced range.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma is comparatively stable across truncations of the RT distribution because it reflects local Gaussian noise rather than tail behavior.

citations.authors: Logan GD, Cowan WB
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: The slowest go-RTs are preferentially inhibited by the stop process, attenuating the right tail of the signal-respond distribution.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: The signal-respond RT distribution is left-shifted and right-truncated relative to go-RT, producing smaller tau in ex-Gaussian fits.

stop.rationale: By the independent horse-race model (Logan & Cowan, 1984), commission errors on stop trials sample the lower portion of the go-RT distribution because they are precisely the trials on which the go process beat the stop process. Empirically, signal-respond (failed-stop) RTs are systematically 40-100 ms faster than correct-go RTs, with reduced tail (smaller tau) because slow go-process trials are preferentially inhibited. Mu shifted ~50 ms below go, tau attenuated to ~75, sigma broadly preserved. This is consistent with the configured failure_rt_cap_fraction=0.85 of go-RT.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go-RTs following stop-signal trials were ~20-40 ms slower than go-RTs following go trials in the validation data.

citations.authors: Bissett PG, Logan GD
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-signal slowing of approximately 25-40 ms was observed regardless of stop success, consistent with reactive control reactivation of the goal set.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD
citations.title: Long-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Memory retrieval, task goals, and cognitive control
citations.quote: Reliable post-stop slowing in the 20-50 ms range emerges on the trial immediately following a stop trial.

citations.authors: Bissett PG, Logan GD
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing was largest on trial N+1 and dissipated by trial N+2 to N+3.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sequential effects on go-RT following stop trials were largely confined to the immediately following trial.

citations.authors: Bissett PG, Logan GD
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sequential slowing was specifically observed on go trials that followed stop trials (both successful and unsuccessful), affecting the upcoming go-RT.

post_stop_slowing.rationale: Post-stop (post-signal) slowing is one of the most reliably documented sequential effects in the stop-signal literature (Verbruggen, Logan, & Stevens, 2008; Bissett & Logan, 2011). After both successful and unsuccessful stop trials, participants slow their go-RT on the subsequent 1-2 trials by ~20-40 ms, reflecting reactive control adjustments and goal reactivation. Enabled with magnitude 25 ms decaying over ~2 trials.

citations.authors: Rabbitt PMA
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Responses on trials immediately following an error were slower than responses following correct trials by approximately 30-60 ms.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HL, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowdown: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Robust post-error slowing of approximately 20-40 ms is observed across choice-RT paradigms when measured with within-subject contrast methods that avoid time-on-task confounds.

citations.authors: Notebaert W, Houtman F, Van Opstal F, Gevers W, Fias W, Verguts T
citations.title: Post-error slowing: an orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes of 25-50 ms were observed across simple choice-RT designs.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HL, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowdown: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing is largest on trial N+1 and dissipates rapidly, often within one to two subsequent trials.

citations.authors: Rabbitt PMA
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT recovery from post-error slowing was essentially complete by the second post-error trial.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HL, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowdown: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing is operationalized as the RT increase on the trial following a choice error relative to the trial following a correct choice.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Standard post-error slowing on choice-RT tasks (Rabbitt, 1966; Dutilh et al., 2012) produces ~20-40 ms RT increases following choice errors. Distinct from post-stop slowing because it is triggered by go-trial choice errors rather than stop trials. Modest magnitude appropriate given that choice errors on this 2AFC task are rare (~3%).

vigilance_decrement.rationale: While time-on-task effects are observable in long stop-signal sessions, the 288-trial STOP-IT protocol (~12-15 min) is below the threshold at which vigilance decrements reliably emerge in healthy adults. Disabled to avoid overfitting; the task is short enough that block-by-block RT shifts are typically within noise.

practice_effects.rationale: STOP-IT includes one practice block (32 trials) before experimental blocks, so most learning has plateaued by trial 33. Within-experimental-phase practice effects are small relative to between-subject variance and are commonly omitted in computational SST models.

proactive_slowing.rationale: Although proactive control (slowing in anticipation of stop signals) is documented for SST, the STOP-IT protocol's fixed 25% stop probability and absence of explicit stop-rate manipulations means proactive adjustments are stable across blocks rather than time-varying. Captured implicitly in the baseline mu rather than as a temporal effect.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SDs based on STOP-IT large-sample validation data and choice-RT meta-analyses. rt_mean_sd_ms ~80 ms reflects typical population-level dispersion of mean go-RT in healthy 18-35 adults. rt_condition_sd_ms ~35 ms captures the additional jitter in the go-vs-stop RT difference (i.e., individual differences in horse-race dynamics). sigma_tau_range [0.75, 1.30] allows distributional shape variability while preserving plausibility (Matzke & Wagenmakers, 2009). accuracy_sd 0.025 reflects narrow ceiling-effect dispersion on go accuracy (~97% mean); stop accuracy is staircase-clamped near 0.50 with very small dispersion. omission_sd 0.01 reflects rare and low-variance attentional lapses.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Across STOP-IT validation samples, between-subject SD of mean go-RT in healthy 18-35 adults is typically 60-100 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge C, Powell G, Sumner P
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go-RT on the stop-signal task in their large sample was approximately 75 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge C, Powell G, Sumner P
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Individual-difference variance in within-task RT contrasts (e.g., go vs. failed-stop) is substantially smaller than variance in mean RT, with SDs typically 25-45 ms.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Variability in the go-vs-signal-respond RT difference across subjects sits in the 25-50 ms range.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Wagenmakers EJ
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Across the surveyed studies, individual ex-Gaussian sigma and tau parameters varied by a factor of roughly 0.7 to 1.3 around the group mean in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy in healthy STOP-IT samples averages ~96-98% with between-subject SD typically 0.02-0.04.

citations.authors: Hedge C, Powell G, Sumner P
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stop-signal go-accuracy ceiling effects produce narrow between-subject dispersion, with SD ~0.025 in their sample.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GP, Beste C, Bissett PG, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates in healthy adults are very low (~1%) with correspondingly small between-subject SDs around 0.01.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Omission errors on go trials were rare and tightly distributed across subjects in the validation sample.
